legal_status_us: Schedule I (federal); decriminalized in some jurisdictions
legal_status_un: Not scheduled under 1971 Convention on Psychotropic Substances (spores controlled in some countries)
notable_research: Griffiths et al. 2006 (psilocybin-occasioned mystical experiences); Carhart-Harris et al. 2016 (neural correlates); MAPS trials for treatment-resistant depression
notes: Formally re-examined in modern clinical trials since 2000s; 'magic mushrooms'

scientific_name: Banisteriopsis caapi + Psychotria viridis (or Diplopterys cabrerana)
traditional_cultures: Shipibo-Conibo, Shuar, Asháninka, numerous Amazonian peoples
traditional_use: Shamanic healing, divination, spiritual communication with plant spirits
preparation_method: Decoction (caapi vine + chacruna leaves boiled into brew)
legal_status_us: DMT is Schedule I; MAOI components not scheduled; ceremonial use recognized for some religious groups in US
legal_status_un: DMT is Schedule I under 1971 Convention; brew itself not explicitly scheduled
first_documented_use_century: Pre-Columbian (estimated 1000+ years; confirmed 1000 CE from archaeological finds)
notable_research: Shanon 2002 (phenomenology); Riba et al. 2003 (pharmacokinetics); Palhano-Fontes et al. 2019 (antidepressant effects)
notes: Combination brew; the MAOI in caapi makes DMT orally active; UNESCO recognized its cultural heritage

traditional_cultures: Huichol (Wixáritari), Tarahumara, Native American Church members (widespread)
traditional_use: Vision quests, healing ceremonies, rites of passage, Native American Church prayer meetings
preparation_method: Dried cactus buttons chewed or prepared as tea
legal_status_us: Schedule I (federal); exempt for Native American Church members under American Indian Religious Freedom Act
legal_status_un: Schedule I under 1971 Convention on Psychotropic Substances
first_documented_use_century: Pre-Columbian (5700 BCE confirmed by radiocarbon dating in Texas)
notable_research: Rouhier 1927; Schultes & Hofmann 1979; Halpern et al. 2005 (long-term effects in NAC members)
notes: One of the oldest documented psychedelic plant uses; Lophophora diffusa also used; mescaline first isolated 1897

traditional_cultures: Andean cultures (Chavín, Chimú, Moche, Inca and successors)
traditional_use: Healing ceremonies (curanderismo), divination, San Pedro ritual
preparation_method: Sliced and boiled into tea (cimora brew); often combined with other plants
legal_status_us: Not explicitly scheduled (mescaline is Schedule I); San Pedro plant itself legal to grow ornamentally in US
legal_status_un: Mescaline scheduled; plant not explicitly listed
first_documented_use_century: Pre-Columbian (2000+ BCE; depicted in Chavín iconography ~1400 BCE)
notes: Named after Saint Peter (gatekeeper of heaven); still used by mestizo healers

geographic_origin: West/Central Africa (Gabon; Cameroon; Republic of Congo)
traditional_use: Initiation rites (Bwiti), healing ceremonies, ancestor communication
preparation_method: Root bark consumed in large doses for initiation; smaller doses for minor ceremonies
legal_status_us: Schedule I (federal US); unscheduled in several countries (e.g., Canada; Mexico; New Zealand)
legal_status_un: Not scheduled under UN conventions; national legislation varies
notable_research: Lotsof 1985 (anti-addictive properties patent); Alper et al. 2001; Noller et al. 2018 (NZ observational study)
notes: Longest single-use ceremonial session of any entheogen (several days); studied for addiction treatment

scientific_name: Mimosa hostilis (Jurema), Acacia confusa, Psychotria viridis (among many others)
traditional_cultures: Jurema cult (Brazil), various Amazonian and Caribbean traditions
legal_status_us: DMT is Schedule I; plants themselves not scheduled federally, though state laws vary
legal_status_un: Schedule I under 1971 Convention on Psychotropic Substances
first_documented_use_century: Estimated pre-Columbian; cohoba snuff documented 1496 CE by Fray Ramón Pané
notable_research: Strassman 2001 (DMT: The Spirit Molecule); Carbonaro & Gatch 2016 (review)
notes: Endogenous to the human body in trace amounts; also found in over 60 plant species; very short duration when smoked

traditional_use: Healing, divination, vision-seeking in shamanic context
preparation_method: Fresh leaves chewed (traditional Mazatec method); leaves smoked (modern)
legal_status_us: Not federally scheduled in US; Schedule I in several states; varies internationally
first_documented_use_century: 20th century (first described by outsiders in 1939; traditional use presumably older)
notable_research: Roth et al. 2002 (kappa-opioid mechanism); Cunningham et al. 2011
notes: Unique kappa-opioid receptor agonist; not a tryptamine or phenethylamine; Mazatec name: 'ska María Pastora'

geographic_origin: Boreal forests, Northern Hemisphere (Siberia; Scandinavia; North America)
traditional_use: Shamanic ritual, religious ecstasy (proposed connection to Vedic Soma and Norse tradition)
preparation_method: Dried caps consumed; urine recycling (traditional Siberian practice); boiled to reduce ibotenic acid
legal_status_us: Not federally scheduled (muscimol and ibotenic acid are not controlled); legal in most US states
first_documented_use_century: Hypothesized ~4000 BCE (contested); Siberian use documented 17th century CE
notes: Iconic red-and-white mushroom; legal status differs from psilocybin mushrooms; muscimol is a GABA agonist

traditional_cultures: Widespread: Vedic India, Scythians, Rastafari, Taoism, Sufi traditions
preparation_method: Smoked, vaporized, edibles, infused oils, bhang (drink)
legal_status_us: Schedule I (federal); legal for medical/recreational use in many US states
legal_status_un: Schedule IV under 1961 Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs (reconsidered by WHO 2019; reclassified 2020)
notes: Devane et al. 1992 (endocannabinoid system); numerous clinical studies for pain, anxiety, nausea
column16: Included here for its historical entheogenic role; legal landscape rapidly evolving

scientific_name: Claviceps purpurea (ergot), Turbina corymbosa, Ipomoea tricolor
traditional_use: Eleusinian Mysteries (kykeon potion), Aztec ritual divination
preparation_method: Ergot alkaloids in grain beverage (kykeon); seeds ground into paste
legal_status_us: LSA is Schedule III (as 'ergine') in US; morning glory plants are legal
legal_status_un: Not explicitly scheduled; LSA analogue laws vary
first_documented_use_century: ~1500 BCE (Eleusinian Mysteries); Aztec use documented 1500s CE
notable_research: Wasson, Hofmann & Ruck 1978 (The Road to Eleusis); Hofmann 1971 (LSA identification)
notes: Hofmann synthesised LSD from ergotamine in 1938; LSD not covered here as purely synthetic

legal_status_un: Schedule I under 1971 Convention on Psychotropic Substances
notable_research: Heffter 1898 (first self-experiment); Klüver 1928 (mescal buttons phenomenology); Aldous Huxley 1954 (The Doors of Perception)
notes: Pure synthetic form distinguished from plant-based sources; included for completeness of mescaline research

traditional_cultures: Claimed Seri and Tohono O'odham (limited evidence), modern neo-shamanic use
traditional_use: Purported ritual use; modern spiritual/therapeutic context
legal_status_us: 5-MeO-DMT is Schedule I (as of 2011) in US
legal_status_un: 5-MeO-DMT not explicitly scheduled under UN conventions; national laws vary
first_documented_use_century: Traditional use pre-modern era is contested; modern documented use 1980s CE
notable_research: Ott 2001; Uthaug et al. 2019 (survey study); Davis et al. 2019 (survey: depression/anxiety)
notes: Also found in numerous plant species (Anadenanthera, Virola); traditional shamanic use specifically from toad venom is archaeologically unverified
